one_line_takeaway: Using an LLM as a constrained prompt optimizer plus noise-copying regeneration, CSI forges tampered-but-still-'watermarked' diffusion images and is the first attack to substantially break the content-aware SEAL watermark (81% ASR where prior attacks get 0-7%), exposing a semantic-level security gap in current semantic watermarking.
key_finding: Against the three content-independent watermarks (Gaussian Shading, Tree-Ring, WIND), all three attacks including CSI reach ~100% ASR, confirming these schemes are trivially forgeable. Against the content-aware SEAL, the two baseline attacks collapse (LFA 0% ASR, RPM 7%), whereas CSI still achieves 81% ASR, raising mean ASR across the four schemes to 95.25% vs 73.45% (LFA) and 76.75% (RPM). Detector-statistic analysis shows CSI keeps forged images comfortably on the 'watermarked' side of each threshold (e.g., SEAL patch matches 134.8 avg vs threshold 12; TRW L1 47.42 avg vs threshold 77). The LLM semantic constraints also preserve semantics better than unconstrained regeneration (FID 178.75 vs RPM 235.40, approaching clean SEAL's 164.27).
core_contribution: The paper introduces CSI (Coherence-Preserving Semantic Injection), described as the first systematic forgery/evasion attack against content-aware semantic image watermarks (CSW), specifically SEAL. Prior semantic watermarks (Tree-Ring, Gaussian Shading, WIND) embed signals only in the initial latent noise with weak semantic alignment and are already known to be forgeable; SEAL was designed to resist this by binding verification to image semantics. CSI's own contribution is a two-part attack pipeline: (i) ASI (Adversarial Semantic Injection), which treats an LLM as a black-box optimizer that proposes a batch of minimally edited prompts preserving global 'anchor' subjects while injecting a target attribute, combined with DDIM inversion and copying of the CSW noise schedule so that detector changes are attributable to semantics rather than sampling noise; and (ii) CHF (Consistency-Based Hierarchical Filtering), a cascade of increasingly expensive acceptance tests (cheap text-only anchor similarity, then BLIP-caption visual-anchor alignment, then CSW noise-image discrepancy) that keeps only candidates that alter local semantics yet still pass the watermark detector.
threat_model: Attacker goal is watermark forgery/evasion: take a legitimately watermarked diffusion image, inject new/altered local semantic attributes (tampering), yet have the modified image still be accepted by the watermark detector as 'watermark present'. The attacker has access to the watermarked image, can run DDIM inversion and re-generate with Stable Diffusion V2, copy the extracted CSW noise schedule, and query a general-purpose LLM (GPT-4o-mini) as a prompt proposer. The attack is framed as black-box with respect to the watermark verifier's decision (thresholds are known/estimated). Success is measured against four watermark defenses, with SEAL being the hard, content-aware target the attack claims to break.
limitations: Very short (4-page) WWW '26 paper with a thin evaluation: a single generator (SD V2), a single LLM (GPT-4o-mini), and one prompt dataset, with no ablations of the CHF thresholds, no reported sample sizes, and no confidence intervals or seeds/variance. SEAL ASR is 81%, not a complete break, and the paper does not analyze the failure cases. Detector 'thresholds' and safety margins are asserted with little justification, and one scheme is inconsistently named (WIND vs 'WINE' in Sec 3.2). FID is used as a proxy for semantic preservation, which is coarse and set-level rather than per-image; there is no human evaluation of whether tampering is perceptually coherent, and no defense/mitigation is proposed or evaluated. Only SEAL represents the content-aware class, so generality of the 'content-aware is broken' claim rests on one scheme.

datasets_used: Stable-Diffusion-Prompts dataset (Gustavosta, HuggingFace) as evaluation prompts
Stable Diffusion V2 as the image generation model
GPT-4o-mini as the LLM prompt optimizer
quantitative_results: ASR vs Gaussian Shading: LFA 100%, RPM 100%, CSI 100%
ASR vs Tree-Ring: LFA 93.81%, RPM 100%, CSI 100%
ASR vs WIND: LFA 100%, RPM 100%, CSI 100%
ASR vs SEAL (content-aware): LFA 0%, RPM 7%, CSI 81%
TRW detection L1 under CSI: avg 47.42, max 57.63, threshold 77.00, safety margin 19.37
SEAL patch matches under CSI: avg 134.8, min 72, threshold 12
GSW matching score under CSI: 1.00 (avg and max), threshold tau_HITS 0.7148
WIND: exact noise match across all attacked samples (complete evasion)
FID vs original set: CSI 178.75, RPM 235.40, clean SEAL 164.27; CSI reduces FID by 56.65 (24.1%) vs RPM and cuts excess FID (+14.5 vs +71.1) by ~80%
baselines_compared: RPM - Regeneration with the Private Model (attack baseline)
SEAL - Semantic-Aware Image Watermarking (content-aware defense target)
